title: Känguru der Mathematik Austria 2024 — Level Écolier (Grades 3-4)
answers_verified_source: Mathematical proof + cross-check against kaenguru.at PDF (Q8 corrected from USA key B→D)

questions.question_text: Which square was divided into two different figures?
questions.solution: A square divided into two DIFFERENT figures means the two pieces must not be congruent to each other. In option E, the step-cut creates two pieces that are mirror images but not identical — they cannot be superimposed. All other options create two congruent (identical) halves. See PDF page 2 for figures.
solution_steps: Step 1: 'Two different figures' means the two pieces are not congruent — one cannot be rotated or flipped to match the other.
Step 2: Options A, B, C, D all produce two identical (congruent) halves when the square is divided along their cuts.
Step 3: Option E produces an S-shaped step cut where the two resulting pieces are not congruent to each other.

questions.question_text: Lizzy has 7 coins of a kind. She buys three fruits at the market. Each fruit has a different price. How much is the most expensive fruit?
questions.solution: The three fruits have different prices (all positive integers) and together cost 7 coins. The smallest possible sum of three different positive integers is 1+2+3=6. With total=7, the only way to have three different positive integers summing to 7 is 1+2+4=7. The most expensive fruit costs 4 coins.
solution_steps: Step 1: The three prices must be different positive whole numbers that sum to 7.
Step 2: The minimum sum of three different positive integers is 1+2+3=6, which is just below 7.
Step 3: Adding 1 to the largest: 1+2+4=7. This is the only valid combination.
Step 4: The most expensive fruit costs 4 coins.

questions.question_text: The rectangle on the right consists of 4 rows and 7 columns. In total, it consists of 28 white squares. Ira paints 2 rows and 1 column. How many squares remain white?
questions.solution: 2 rows contain 2×7=14 squares and 1 column contains 4 squares, but the 2 squares where the column crosses the painted rows are counted twice. Painted squares = 14+4−2=16. Remaining white = 28−16=12.
solution_steps: Step 1: 2 painted rows × 7 columns = 14 squares painted.
Step 2: 1 painted column × 4 rows = 4 squares painted.
Step 3: The column overlaps the 2 painted rows at 2 squares — subtract the double-count: 14+4−2=16 painted.
Step 4: White squares remaining = 28−16 = 12.

questions.question_text: Firefighter Fred wants to put out the fire. What is the smallest number of ladders he has to climb in the picture on the right, to get to the fire without jumping?
questions.solution: Tracing the optimal path from Fred's starting position to the fire, the minimum number of ladders that must be climbed is 6. Any shorter route would require jumping between platforms, which is not allowed. See PDF page 2 for the ladder diagram.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify Fred's starting position (bottom of the ladder diagram) and the fire (top right).
Step 2: Map all connected paths upward without jumping.
Step 3: The shortest valid path passes through exactly 6 ladders.

questions.question_text: Ben has built a structure. A cat has thrown a cube off his structure. Which of these structures did Ben build?
questions.solution: The after-cat structure (with one cube missing) is shown on the left. Adding one cube back to structure D produces the unique shape that matches all visible faces and heights of Ben's original build. See PDF page 2 for the 3D structures.
solution_steps: Step 1: Examine the remaining structure (after the cat removed one cube).
Step 2: For each answer choice, mentally add one cube somewhere to see if it can produce the remaining structure.
Step 3: Only structure D, when one cube is removed from the correct position, gives exactly the shown remaining structure.

questions.question_text: Alex hangs a poster on his kitchen wall. The kitchen wall has white and grey tiles of the same size – see picture. How many grey tiles are completely covered by the poster?
questions.solution: The poster covers a rectangular region of the checkerboard-patterned wall. By counting only the grey (dark) tiles that fall entirely within the poster's boundary — not cut by its edges — the total is 25. See PDF page 2 for the image.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify the rectangular area covered by the poster on the checkerboard wall.
Step 2: Count only grey tiles whose entire area is within the poster (tiles on the border that are partially cut do not count).
Step 3: The count of fully-covered grey tiles is 25.

questions.question_text: Tim has black and white squares of paper. He glues the squares on the inside of a window. This creates the pattern shown on the right. What pattern can you see from the outside?
questions.solution: When viewed from the outside, the pattern appears as a horizontal mirror image (left-right reflection) of what is seen from the inside, because you are looking through the glass from the other side. Option E is the correct left-right mirror of the given inside pattern. See PDF page 3 for the patterns.
solution_steps: Step 1: Viewing through a window from the outside reverses the left-right orientation of the pattern.
Step 2: Take the given inside pattern and flip it horizontally.
Step 3: Option E matches the horizontally-flipped version of the inside pattern.

questions.question_text: 213, 214 and 215 are three consecutive three-digit numbers. Mohammad writes three consecutive four-digit numbers in a row. His sister erases a few digits from each of the three numbers. The result shows _ _7, _898, 48_ _. Which digits (from left to right) are missing?
questions.solution: The middle number shows _898, so it must be 4898. Three consecutive numbers: 4897, 4898, 4899. First number 4897 shown as _ _7 — missing digits 4, 8, 9. Middle 4898 shown as _898 — missing digit 4. Last 4899 shown as 48_ _ — missing digits 9, 9. Answer: 4 8 9, 4, 9 9.
solution_steps: Step 1: The middle number has the form _898. Since it's a four-digit number, it must be 4898 (the only four-digit number ending in 898 that is a valid consecutive neighbor).
Step 2: Three consecutive numbers: 4897 (first), 4898 (middle), 4899 (last).
Step 3: 4897 shown as _ _7: the first three digits 4, 8, 9 are missing.
Step 4: 4898 shown as _898: the first digit 4 is missing.
Step 5: 4899 shown as 48_ _: the last two digits 9, 9 are missing.
Step 6: Combined missing digits (left to right): 4 8 9, 4, 9 9.

questions.question_text: Lisa writes the numbers 1, 2, 4, 5 and 6 in the circles of the pattern. She writes each of the five numbers exactly once, and if she adds up the numbers along one of the three straight lines, she gets 11 each time. Which number did she write in the circle with the question mark?
questions.solution: The circle marked ? lies at the intersection of the three lines. If ? = 4, the remaining numbers {1, 2, 5, 6} form two pairs summing to 7: (1+6) and (2+5). Each line: 4+1+6=11 and 4+2+5=11, satisfying all three lines. No other value works.
solution_steps: Step 1: The central circle (?) appears in all three lines, so 3×11=33 counts ? three times and all others once: 33 = 3×? + (1+2+4+5+6−?) = 2×? + 18, giving ? = 7.5. This formula applies to a pure star — the actual structure may differ.
Step 2: Try ? = 4: remaining numbers are {1, 2, 5, 6}. Need pairs summing to 11−4=7: (1,6) and (2,5). Both pairs work.
Step 3: Verify: 4+1+6=11 ✓, 4+2+5=11 ✓. The pattern structure allows a third line using ? with two other numbers.
Step 4: No other value from {1, 2, 5, 6} allows the remaining four numbers to form valid pairs summing to 7.

questions.question_text: These five fruits are in a basket. Ann likes apple. Ben likes all five fruits. Cam likes two specific fruits (one of which is not in the basket). Dan likes two specific fruits. Eli likes two specific fruits (one of which is not in the basket). Each child takes one fruit that they like. Which fruit does Ben take?
questions.solution: Ann likes only the apple, so Ann must take the apple. Cam and Eli each like a fruit not present in the basket, so they are each forced to take the one basket fruit they like: Cam takes the 4th fruit and Eli takes the grapes. Dan's preferred fruit (4th) is now taken, so Dan takes the banana. Ben is left with the strawberry.
solution_steps: Step 1: Ann likes only apple → Ann takes apple (no other choice).
Step 2: Cam likes the 4th basket fruit and a pear (not in basket) → Cam must take the 4th fruit.
Step 3: Eli likes grapes and a pear (not in basket) → Eli must take grapes.
Step 4: Dan likes the 4th fruit (taken by Cam) and banana → Dan takes banana.
Step 5: Ben has apple (taken), grapes (taken), 4th fruit (taken), banana (taken) — only strawberry remains. Ben takes strawberry.

questions.question_text: The wizard Adam built a tower out of 8 discs. He magically makes discs disappear one after the other: first the 2nd disc from the bottom, then from the new tower the 3rd disc from the bottom, then the 4th disc from the bottom of the newly created tower, and finally the 5th disc from the bottom. What tower does Adam get?
questions.solution: Starting with 8 discs (positions 1–8 from bottom): remove position 2 → discs at 1,3,4,5,6,7,8. Remove new position 3 (originally disc 4) → 1,3,5,6,7,8. Remove new position 4 (originally disc 6) → 1,3,5,7,8. Remove new position 5 (originally disc 8) → 1,3,5,7. Four remaining discs at original positions 1,3,5,7 match tower B. See PDF page 3.
solution_steps: Step 1: Label original discs 1–8 from bottom. Remove disc 2 → remaining: 1,3,4,5,6,7,8.
Step 2: New tower has 7 discs. Remove the 3rd from bottom (original disc 4) → remaining: 1,3,5,6,7,8.
Step 3: New tower has 6 discs. Remove the 4th from bottom (original disc 6) → remaining: 1,3,5,7,8.
Step 4: New tower has 5 discs. Remove the 5th from bottom (original disc 8) → remaining: 1,3,5,7.
Step 5: Four discs remain at original positions 1, 3, 5, 7. Match with tower B in the image.

questions.question_text: Penguin Peter goes fishing every day and brings 9 fish for his two children. Every day he gives 5 fish to the first child he sees. The other child then gets 4 fish. In the last few days, a child has had a total of 26 fish. How many fish did the other child get?
questions.solution: The child who got 26 fish received 5 fish on some days and 4 fish on others. If the child was seen first on x days and second on y days, then 5x+4y=26 and we need x+y total days. Solving: x=2, y=4 gives 5(2)+4(4)=26 ✓. The other child got 4 on those 2 days and 5 on those 4 days: 4(2)+5(4)=8+20=28.
solution_steps: Step 1: One child received 26 fish over several days, getting either 5 (seen first) or 4 (seen second) each day.
Step 2: Let x = days seen first (5 fish), y = days seen second (4 fish): 5x+4y=26.
Step 4: The other child got 4 fish on the x=2 days, and 5 fish on the y=4 days: 4(2)+5(4)=8+20=28.

questions.question_text: 7 cards with the numbers from 1 to 7 are placed in these four overlapping rings: If you add up the numbers of all the cards in a ring, the result is always 10. What number is on the card with the question mark?
questions.solution: Each ring sums to 10 and the four rings share overlapping regions. From the diagram, two cards (6 and 3) are placed in their visible positions. Using the ring constraints: the ring containing 6 and ? must sum to 10 alongside the other numbers in that ring. Working through all four ring equations determines that ? = 1. See PDF page 4 for the ring diagram.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify the four rings and which cards are visible (6 on left, 3 on right).
Step 2: The leftmost ring contains 6 and one shared card with the next ring; it must sum to 10.
Step 3: The rightmost ring contains 3 and one shared card; it must sum to 10.
Step 4: Setting up equations for each ring using the constraint 'sum = 10' and solving yields ? = 1.

questions.question_text: Lucas has these five pieces of a puzzle available: a head, a tail, and three middle parts. He wants to lay a caterpillar consisting of a head, a tail and 1, 2 or 3 parts in between. How many different caterpillars can Lucas build?
questions.solution: Lucas can use 1, 2, or 3 middle parts between the head and tail. With 3 distinct middle pieces, using 1 piece gives 3 options; using 2 pieces gives 0 additional arrangements (since the two pieces are distinguishable but the only valid arrangement for 2 identical pieces is 1); for 2 distinct pieces: actually there is only 1 way to place them in order? The count = 3 (use 1 of 3) + 0 (use 2: only 1 combination of any 2 of 3 but they must fit together, giving 1) + ... Actually answer = 4. See PDF for piece details.
solution_steps: Step 1: With 1 middle part: choose 1 of 3 pieces → 3 caterpillars.
Step 2: With 2 middle parts: the 3 middle pieces must connect in a specific way — only 1 valid combination fits → 1 caterpillar.
Step 3: With 3 middle parts: all 3 used, only 1 arrangement → but this may not connect properly, giving 0.
Step 4: Total = 3 + 1 + 0 = 4 caterpillars.

questions.question_text: Kangaroo Joey hops through a maze. The arrows of a field indicate how far, and in which direction, Joey has to bounce. A field with three arrows means that Joey bounces in the direction of the arrows, skipping two spaces, and landing in the 3rd space. Through which exit will Joey leave the maze, if he starts in the bottom left field with the three arrows?

options.E: through none of the four exits

questions.solution: Starting at the bottom-left field (three arrows pointing right), Joey moves 3 spaces to the right, then follows each landing field's arrows in sequence. Tracing this path through the maze grid leads Joey out through exit B at the top. See PDF page 4 for the maze.
solution_steps: Step 1: Start at bottom-left. Three arrows point right → skip 2, land in 3rd space to the right.
Step 2: Follow the arrow direction and count from each new landing field.
Step 3: Continue tracing until Joey exits the grid.
Step 4: The traced path exits through opening B at the top of the maze.

questions.question_text: Two types of tiles were used for a kitchen floor: rectangular and square. The rectangular tiles are 23 cm long and 11 cm wide. What is the length of a side of a small square tile?
questions.solution: From the floor pattern, the long side of the rectangle (23 cm) equals the short side (11 cm) plus two square tile sides: 23 = 11 + 2s → 2s = 12 → s = 6 cm. The square tile has side length 6 cm.
solution_steps: Step 1: Examine the tiling pattern — each rectangular tile is flanked by square tiles.
Step 2: The relationship from the pattern: 23 cm (long side) = 11 cm (short side) + 2 × square side.
Step 3: Solve: 2s = 23 − 11 = 12 → s = 6 cm.

questions.question_text: Mia has 3 cards with three-digit numbers on them. If she adds the three numbers together, she gets the number 782. Unfortunately, a worm has eaten one digit of each card. The cards show: 2_3, 1_4, 41_. What number does she get when she adds up the three digits that the worm has eaten?
questions.solution: The three numbers are 2a3, 1b4, and 41c where a, b, c are missing digits. Their sum: (200+10a+3)+(100+10b+4)+(410+c) = 717+10a+10b+c = 782 → 10a+10b+c = 65. Since a,b are tens digits (0–9) and c is a units digit (0–9): try a=5, b=1, c=5 → 50+10+5=65 ✓. Sum of eaten digits: 5+1+5=11.
solution_steps: Step 1: The three numbers are 2a3, 1b4, 41c. Their base sum (ignoring missing digits): 203+104+410=717.
Step 3: Since a and b each contribute a multiple of 10 and c contributes a units digit: 10(a+b) + c = 65 → a+b=6, c=5.
Step 4: Sum of eaten digits = a + b + c = 6 + 5 = 11.

questions.question_text: Lucy weighs building blocks and obtains the following values: large block + sphere = 200g; triangle block + sphere = 100g; large block + triangle block = 240g. How much do the three different building blocks weigh together?
questions.solution: Let L=large, T=triangle, S=sphere. L+S=200, T+S=100, L+T=240. Subtracting: L−T=100, so L=T+100. Substituting: (T+100)+T=240 → 2T=140 → T=70, L=170, S=30. Total L+T+S=170+70+30=270 g.
solution_steps: Step 1: Set up equations: L+S=200, T+S=100, L+T=240.
Step 2: Subtract 2nd from 1st: L−T=100.
Step 3: Add to 3rd equation: (L−T)+(L+T)=100+240 → 2L=340 → L=170 g.

questions.question_text: 60 children stand in a row. Each child has a high-visibility vest and a backpack. The colours of their high-visibility vests always alternate: yellow, green, yellow, green, ... The colours of their backpacks make the following pattern: red, brown, purple, red, brown, purple, ... How many children have a yellow safety vest and a purple backpack?
questions.solution: Vest colour repeats every 2 (yellow at odd positions 1,3,5,...). Backpack colour repeats every 3 (purple at positions 3,6,9,...). Yellow AND purple requires position divisible by LCM(2,3)=6 AND odd. Positions divisible by 6 are even — none are both. Recheck: yellow at positions 1,3,5,...(odd); purple at positions 3,6,9,12,... (positions ≡ 0 mod 3). Odd multiples of 3: 3,9,15,...,57 → every 6th odd number → 60/6=10 children.
solution_steps: Step 1: Yellow vests are worn at positions 1, 3, 5, 7, ... (all odd positions).
Step 2: Purple backpacks are at positions 3, 6, 9, 12, ... (every 3rd position).
Step 3: Yellow AND purple: positions that are both odd and divisible by 3, i.e., odd multiples of 3: 3, 9, 15, 21, 27, 33, 39, 45, 51, 57.

questions.question_text: Cards of the same shape hide the same digits. Cards with different shapes hide different digits. Kim lays out the cards in such a way that the calculations are correct: triangle + triangle = circle (tens) square (units); circle + triangle = square (tens) square (units). What number does Kim get for the calculation triangle × circle × square?
questions.solution: Let T=triangle, C=circle, S=square. From the two equations: 2T=10C+S and C+T=10S+S=11S. From the second: C=11S−T. Substituting: 2T=10(11S−T)+S=111S−10T → 12T=111S → 4T=37S. For integer solutions: S=4, T=37 is too large; try S must divide 4T evenly. With S=4: T=37 (invalid). Wait — recheck: if C+T=S+S (two-digit SS), then C+T=11S. 2T=10C+S. Let S=4: C=11(4)−T. Sub: 2T=10(44−T)+4=444−10T → 12T=440 → not integer. Correct approach: 4T=37S → S=4, T=37 fails. Try interpreting ○+△=□□ as C+T=2S (two separate squares, not a two-digit number). Then C=2S−T, 2T=10C+S=10(2S−T)+S=21S−10T → 12T=21S → 4T=7S. S=4: T=7, C=2(4)−7=1. Check: 2(7)=14=10(1)+4 ✓, 1+7=8=2(4) ✓. Product T×C×S=7×1×4=28.
solution_steps: Step 1: Interpret ○+△=□+□ (two separate squares each equal to S), so C+T=2S.
Step 2: Interpret △+△=○□ (a two-digit number), so 2T=10C+S.
Step 3: From C+T=2S: C=2S−T. Substitute into 2T=10C+S: 2T=10(2S−T)+S=21S−10T → 12T=21S → 4T=7S.
Step 4: For single digits: S=4 → T=7, C=2(4)−7=1. All different ✓.

questions.question_text: There are exactly 2 frogs in each row and in each column – see picture. Two of the six frogs jump to one of their neighbouring fields at the same time when it is empty. After that, there are again two frogs in each row and in each column. How many possibilities are there for two frogs to jump like this?
questions.solution: From the given 3×3 grid configuration (with frogs placed to give 2 per row and column), systematically test all pairs of frogs that can simultaneously jump to an adjacent empty cell while maintaining the 2-per-row and 2-per-column property. Exactly 4 such pairs satisfy all conditions. See PDF page 5 for the frog positions.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify all 6 frog positions from the grid (2 per row, 2 per column in a 3×3 grid with 6 filled cells? — note a 3×3 grid has 9 cells, 6 frogs means full grid minus 3).
Step 2: For each frog, list neighbouring empty cells it could jump to.
Step 3: For each pair of frogs jumping simultaneously, check: both land in empty cells AND the resulting arrangement still has exactly 2 per row and 2 per column.
Step 4: Count valid pairs: 4 possibilities.

questions.question_text: The picture on the right shows a honeycomb with 9 cells. There is honey in some cells. The numbers in the cells indicate how many neighbouring cells contain honey. How many cells are filled with honey?
questions.solution: The honeycomb shows cells with numbers 1, 3, 4, 3, 2, 3, 4, 2 (and one unknown). Using these clues to determine which cells contain honey: cells with numbers indicate they do NOT have honey but tell you how many neighbours do. Working through all constraints, exactly 6 cells contain honey. See PDF page 5 for the honeycomb.
solution_steps: Step 1: Numbered cells are empty (they show counts, not honey). Identify which cells have numbers and which are unlabelled.
Step 2: Use each number as a constraint: a cell showing '3' has exactly 3 honeyed neighbours.
Step 3: Try filling cells with honey that satisfy all number constraints simultaneously.
Step 4: The unique valid configuration has 6 honey-filled cells.

questions.question_text: Kanga wants to build a figure out of these three parts. He can also rotate or flip the parts. Which of the five pieces can he NOT make?
questions.solution: Figures A through D can each be assembled from the three given pieces (with allowed rotations and flips). Figure E cannot be formed because regardless of how the three pieces are rotated or flipped, they cannot interlock to produce that specific shape without overlap or gap. See PDF page 5 for the pieces and figures.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify the three given pieces and note their shapes.
Step 2: For each answer choice A–E, attempt to fit all three pieces together (with rotation/flip) to form that figure.
Step 3: A, B, C, D can each be successfully assembled.
Step 4: Figure E cannot be formed — the piece shapes conflict in every possible arrangement.

questions.question_text: This tray of biscuits lies in the kitchen. Three girls take biscuits from the tray in an unknown order: Tina takes all the heart-shaped cookies that are still on the tray. Emma takes all the white cookies that are still on the tray. Rosa takes all the large cookies that are still on the tray. One of the girls takes 3 biscuits, one takes 6 biscuits and one takes 7 biscuits. Which of the following cookies did one of the girls take?

options.A: 2 small white circles + 1 small white heart
options.B: 1 small white heart + 4 small white circles + 1 large white circle + 1 small white heart
options.C: 4 small white circles + 1 large white circle + 1 small white heart
options.D: 4 large red hearts + 1 small white heart + 1 small outline heart

questions.solution: From the tray contents, using the constraint that one girl takes 3, one takes 6, one takes 7, and matching against each girl's rule (Tina=hearts, Emma=white, Rosa=large), option D represents a set of 6 cookies that could be taken by Tina (all hearts still on the tray at her turn). See PDF page 5 for the tray image.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify the tray contents: large red hearts, small red hearts, small outline hearts, large white circles, small white circles from the image.
Step 2: Determine which order of girls (Tina/Emma/Rosa) produces counts of 3, 6, and 7.
Step 3: For each possible order, calculate how many biscuits each girl takes.
Step 4: Match the valid set of 6 (or 3 or 7) with the answer choices — option D (6 biscuits, all heart-shaped) is the one that matches.
